INTERVIEW EXPERIENCES
1. Google Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Google | Software Engineer | L3 | Medium
Skills: Java, Data Structures, Graphs, Complexity Analysis
Focus: Algorithmic Efficiency & Edge Case Handling
Interview Experience
The process began with a 45-minute phone screen featuring a string manipulation problem that
evolved into a sliding window challenge. The onsite consisted of four technical rounds and one
"Googliness" round. The technical problems were heavily focused on graphs—specifically
finding the shortest path in a 2D grid with obstacles and implementing a custom iterator for a
nested list. One round pushed for a O(N) solution where a O(N log N) sorting approach
seemed obvious, forcing me to think about frequency arrays. Takeaway: Communication is as
vital as the code. Google interviewers want to see how you react to constraints like "What if the
data is too large for memory?" Be vocal about your trade-offs between time and space
complexity, and always dry-run your code with edge cases before saying you're finished.
2. Google Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Google | Backend Engineer | L4 | Hard
Skills: Go, Distributed Systems, gRPC, Multithreading
Focus: Scalability & Concurrency
Interview Experience
Beyond the standard DSA rounds, there was a heavy emphasis on backend architecture. I was
asked to design a globally distributed rate-limiting service. We spent significant time discussing
the trade-offs between using a centralized Redis store versus local in-memory caching to
reduce latency. The coding rounds used Go and focused on concurrency primitives like
channels and mutexes to build a thread-safe task scheduler. The interviewer pushed me on
"thundering herd" problems and ensuring graceful shutdowns of worker pools. Takeaway:
Focus on low-level system concepts and how they scale. Knowing how a database handles
locking under high load or how gRPC handles streaming was a recurring theme. Don't just
provide a working solution; provide the most optimized one for a billion users.

3. Google Data Scientist Interview Experience
Metadata: Google | Data Scientist | University Grad | Medium
Skills: Python, Statistics, SQL, ML Theory
Focus: Experimental Design & Hypothesis Testing
Interview Experience
The interview consisted of a technical screen followed by five onsite rounds. It wasn't just about
building models; there was a massive focus on A/B testing and product intuition. I was asked to
explain how to measure the success of a new UI feature in Search using specific metrics like
CTR and session length. Technical rounds covered the math behind Bayes' Theorem, linear
regression assumptions, and a SQL round with complex joins and window functions to find the
"Top N users per category." Takeaway: Don't neglect basic stats and the "Why" behind the
math. Being able to explain P-values or the bias-variance tradeoff to a non-technical
stakeholder is a common behavioral-technical hybrid check.
4. Amazon SDE Interview Experience
Metadata: Amazon | SDE | SDE-1 | Medium
Skills: C++, OOP, Linked Lists, Leadership Principles
Focus: Coding Speed & Amazon LPs
Interview Experience
The Online Assessment (OA) had two medium-level DSA questions and a workplace
simulation. The onsite was three rounds. Every single round started with 20 minutes of
"Leadership Principles" (LPs) questions, which are taken very seriously. Technical questions
were standard but required high accuracy—think "Top K Frequent Elements" and "LRU Cache
implementation." I had to explain my design using Object-Oriented principles, ensuring
encapsulation and modularity. Takeaway: You cannot over-prepare for LPs. Use the STAR
method for every behavioral answer and tie it back to "Customer Obsession" or "Ownership." If
your code is perfect but you fail to show backbone or deliver results in your stories, you won't
get the offer.
5. Amazon Backend Engineer Interview Experience

Metadata: Amazon | Backend Engineer | SDE-2 | Hard
Skills: Java, DynamoDB, Microservices, System Design
Focus: Customer Obsession & Architectural Trade-offs
Interview Experience
This was a 4-round virtual onsite. The System Design round focused on designing a "Flash
Sale" system that could handle millions of concurrent requests during a Black Friday event. We
spent a lot of time on database sharding, caching layers (DAX), and using message queues like
SQS to decouple the order processing service from the payment service. The coding rounds
included a hard Dynamic Programming problem related to inventory optimization. Takeaway:
Focus on horizontal scaling and failure recovery. Amazon loves it when you mention "retry logic
with exponential backoff" and "idempotency keys" for payment APIs. Always tie your design
decisions back to the user's experience—latency is a dealbreaker.
6. Amazon Data Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Amazon | Data Engineer | L5 | Medium
Skills: SQL, Python, Redshift, ETL Pipelines
Focus: Data Modeling & Warehouse Architecture
Interview Experience
The interview rounds focused on building scalable ETL pipelines. I was given a scenario where
raw log data was arriving in S3 in various formats and had to design a schema for a star-
schema warehouse in Redshift. The SQL round was intense, involving recursive CTEs and
heavy window functions to calculate year-over-year growth per category. We also discussed
data quality checks and how to handle late-arriving data. Takeaway: Know the difference
between OLTP and OLAP inside out. Understanding when to use partitioning versus sorting
keys in Redshift is a major plus. Being able to visualize the data flow from source to dashboard
is crucial.
7. Microsoft Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Microsoft | Software Engineer | SDE-1 | Medium
Skills: C#, Azure, Trees, Arrays

Focus: Problem Solving & Code Quality
Interview Experience
Microsoft's process felt very "developer-centric" and collaborative. Rounds involved coding on a
shared editor focusing on clean code, proper variable naming, and modularity. Questions were
mostly around Binary Trees (e.g., "Serialize and Deserialize a Binary Tree") and String
manipulation using Stacks. There was also a unique "Debugging" round, where I had to find a
logical error in a provided snippet related to multi-threading. Takeaway: They value candidates
who write readable, maintainable code over "clever" one-liners. Don't use single-letter variables
like i  or j  unless they are loop counters. Mentioning familiarity with Azure services or C#
features like LINQ can give you an edge, even if the role is language-agnostic.
8. Microsoft Full Stack Developer Interview Experience
Metadata: Microsoft | Full Stack Developer | L62 | Medium
Skills: TypeScript, React, .NET Core, System Design
Focus: End-to-End Feature Development
Interview Experience
The technical rounds were split between building a responsive React component with complex
state management and a backend API design. I had to build a "Real-time Task Management" UI
and explain how the frontend would sync with a backend using WebSockets for live updates.
We discussed state persistence, error handling on the client side, and RESTful API best
practices for the backend. Takeaway: Focus on the "Bridge" between front and back.
Understanding how to handle API errors gracefully in the UI—like using skeletons or toast
notifications—is a key focus area. They want to see that you can own a feature from the
database to the browser.
9. Microsoft Data Scientist Interview Experience
Metadata: Microsoft | Data Scientist | Senior | Hard
Skills: Python, PyTorch, Statistics, NLP
Focus: Large Language Models & Mathematical Foundations
Interview Experience

This was a heavy deep-dive into NLP and Transformer architectures. I was asked to walk
through the "Self-Attention" mechanism in detail, explaining why we use scaling factors in dot-
product attention. Another round focused on the math behind gradient descent and optimization
algorithms like Adam versus SGD. We also discussed "Model Explainability" (SHAP/LIME) for a
credit scoring use case. Takeaway: For senior roles, they expect you to know the underlying
calculus and linear algebra, not just how to call a library. Be prepared to derive basic loss
functions on a whiteboard and discuss the trade-offs of different activation functions in deep
networks.
10. Meta Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Meta | Software Engineer | E4 | Hard
Skills: C++, Algorithms, System Design
Focus: Speed & Optimal DSA Solutions
Interview Experience
Meta is famous for its high-speed coding expectations. You typically have 45 minutes to solve
two LeetCode-style problems perfectly. My questions were "Lowest Common Ancestor of a
Binary Tree" and a variation of "Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum." There was zero room for
logical errors. The System Design round focused on "Designing the Instagram News Feed,"
where we discussed pull vs. push models for updates and caching strategies for high-celebrity
accounts. Takeaway: Speed is everything. Practice until you can solve LeetCode Medium
problems in under 15 minutes. During the design round, focus on bottleneck identification—how
do you handle a user with 100 million followers? Scaling for "Big Data" is the only thing that
matters here.
11. Uber Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Uber | Backend Engineer | L4 | Hard
Skills: Go, Distributed Systems, Geospatial Indexing, Kafka
Interview Experience
This was an extremely technical experience. One round was dedicated entirely to
"Concurrency" where I had to implement a thread-safe worker pool from scratch using Go
channels. The System Design round was the highlight: "Designing Uber Ride Dispatch." We

discussed Quad-trees and Google’s H3 indexing for location data to find the nearest drivers in
sub-millisecond time. We also touched upon how Kafka handles event-driven updates for car
locations. Takeaway: Uber loves candidates who understand the "Real-time" nature of their
business. Knowing how to handle high-write throughput and geospatial queries is essential.
Brush up on distributed locking and how to handle network partitions in a CAP-theorem context.
12. Uber Data Scientist Interview Experience
Metadata: Uber | Data Scientist | L3 | Medium
Skills: Python, R, Experimentation, Pricing Models
Interview Experience
The focus was heavily on "Marketplace Dynamics." Questions involved surge pricing algorithms
and how to reduce "churn" for drivers using incentive structures. I had to solve a probability
problem regarding wait times and driver supply-demand gaps. A large part of the onsite was a
case study on A/B testing a new route-sharing feature, where I had to define the "North Star"
metric and identify potential "interference" effects. Takeaway: Understand Uber’s two-sided
marketplace model before the interview. They care more about how you think about business
trade-offs (e.g., higher price vs. lower wait time) than just your ability to tune a random forest.
13. Airbnb Full Stack Developer Interview Experience
Metadata: Airbnb | Full Stack | L5 | Medium
Skills: React, GraphQL, CSS-in-JS, UX Design
Interview Experience
Airbnb’s "Craft" round is legendary. You are given a Figma design and asked to implement it in
React, focusing on pixel perfection, accessibility (ARIA labels), and responsive layouts. They
also have a unique "Culture" round with non-engineers to ensure you align with their values. In
the backend round, I designed a GraphQL schema for a "Booking Management" system,
discussing the "N+1 problem" and how to solve it using DataLoaders. Takeaway: They care
about the "Host" mindset. Build with empathy and focus on the user journey. Don't just make
the code work; make the UI feel smooth and intentional. Performance profiling (Lighthouse
scores) was a major talking point.

14. Airbnb Data Analyst Interview Experience
Metadata: Airbnb | Data Analyst | Junior | Medium
Skills: SQL, Tableau, Business Metrics, Storytelling
Interview Experience
This role focused heavily on "Data Storytelling." I was given a dataset of bookings and had to
identify why a certain city’s revenue was dropping despite high traffic. The SQL rounds were
fairly standard (Joins, Aggregations), but the presentation round was key—I had to explain my
findings to a mock "Product Manager." We discussed "Seasonality" and "Cannibalization" of
listings. Takeaway: Focus on the "So what?" of the data. Don't just present charts; present
actionable insights. "Revenue is down by 5%" is a stat; "Revenue is down because local taxes
increased, so we should offer host incentives" is an insight.
15. Netflix Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Netflix | Backend Engineer | Senior | Hard
Skills: Java, Spring Boot, Chaos Engineering, Cassandra
Interview Experience
Netflix usually only hires senior engineers, and the interview reflects that. There was no
traditional LeetCode; instead, it was a deep dive into my past projects and "Cultural Fit." They
asked how I would handle a service failure in the middle of the night and my thoughts on
"Chaos Monkey" testing. We discussed the architecture of a content delivery network (CDN)
and how to ensure 99.99% uptime for streaming. Takeaway: Read the Netflix Culture Memo
multiple times. Be opinionated but flexible. They look for "Stunning Colleagues" who are self-
driven. If you need a manager to tell you what to do, you won't fit in. Be ready to discuss "High
Alignment, Low Coupling."
16. Stripe Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Stripe | Software Engineer | L3 | Medium
Skills: Ruby, API Design, Integration, Webhooks
Interview Experience

Stripe focuses on "Work Sample" tests rather than brain teasers. I had to integrate a new
payment method into a mock API and handle webhook notifications for successful payments.
They look for how you read their documentation and handle edge cases like idempotency and
network timeouts. The system design round focused on building a "Global Ledger" that is
consistent across regions. Takeaway: They value "Clean Code" and excellent documentation
skills. Treat your interview code as if it’s going straight to production. Commenting on why you
chose a specific error code (e.g., 400 vs 422) shows a level of maturity they highly value in their
engineers.
17. Atlassian Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Atlassian | Software Engineer | P3 | Medium
Skills: Java, React, Jira API, Microservices
Interview Experience
Atlassian places huge weight on their "Values Interview." If you don't pass the values check (like
"Don't #@!% the customer"), the technical score doesn't matter. Technical rounds included a
"System Design" of a collaborative document editor like Confluence, focusing on operational
transformation or CRDTs for real-time syncing. Coding was standard but required high-quality
unit tests. Takeaway: Use the word "Feedback" often and show that you are a team player.
They are big on open communication and collaborative problem solving. Be ready to admit
when you don't know something; they value honesty over "faking it."
18. Atlassian Frontend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Atlassian | Frontend Engineer | P2 | Medium
Skills: JavaScript, React, Testing Library, Webpack
Interview Experience
The focus was on "Frontend System Design"—designing a component library that could be
shared across Jira and Trello. I had to explain how to manage state globally versus locally and
how to optimize bundle sizes for slow networks. The coding round involved building a "Drag and
Drop" interface for a Kanban board using vanilla JS or React. Takeaway: Know your React
Hooks and testing strategies (Jest/Cypress) inside out. They love candidates who think about
"Micro-frontends" and how to scale a UI across multiple large teams without breaking things.

19. Adobe Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Adobe | Software Engineer | Entry | Medium
Skills: C++, Data Structures, OS, Graphics Algorithms
Interview Experience
Strong focus on Computer Science fundamentals. Questions on memory management,
pointers, and multithreading were very common. One round involved optimizing a simple image
processing algorithm to run in parallel. Coding questions involved Linked Lists and Stacks, but
with a twist on memory efficiency. Takeaway: Refresh your OS and DBMS concepts. Adobe
builds heavy software like Photoshop and Premiere, so they care about how your code affects
the CPU and RAM. Knowing how a "Buffer" works or how "Garbage Collection" impacts
performance is a major plus for their core engineering roles.
20. Salesforce Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Salesforce | Backend Engineer | AMTS | Medium
Skills: Java, Apex, Multi-tenancy, Cloud Architecture
Interview Experience
The interview focused on "Multi-tenant Architecture." I was asked how to keep one client's data
separate from another's when they share the same physical database. Coding was standard
LeetCode Medium, but with a heavy emphasis on "Design Patterns" like Singleton, Factory, and
Strategy. We also discussed how Salesforce handles "Governor Limits" to prevent one user
from hogging all system resources. Takeaway: Understand the SaaS business model and
architectural patterns. They want to see that you understand the complexity of building a
"Platform" that other developers build on top of, not just a standalone app.
21. Swiggy Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Swiggy | Backend Engineer | SDE-2 | Medium
Skills: Java, Redis, Kafka, Microservices
Interview Experience

Questions focused on "High Concurrency" and "Low Latency." How do you handle 100k orders
per minute during a cricket match? We discussed using Redis for real-time tracking and Kafka
for event-driven order status updates. I had to implement a custom "Rate Limiter" in the coding
round. Takeaway: Know your caching strategies (Write-through vs Write-back). Swiggy deals
with "perishable" data, so understanding how to invalidate caches and manage real-time
streams is key to passing their senior rounds.
22. Swiggy Data Scientist Interview Experience
Metadata: Swiggy | Data Scientist | Medium
Skills: Python, XGBoost, Logistics Optimization, Forecasting
Interview Experience
The focus was on "Last-mile Delivery Optimization." I was asked to design a model to predict
the "Time to Deliver" (ETA) by factoring in weather, traffic, and restaurant preparation time. We
discussed feature engineering for time-series data and how to handle outliers (like a sudden
rainstorm). Takeaway: Focus on practical applications of ML. They aren't looking for the most
"complex" model, but the one that is most "interpretable" and "reliable" for their delivery fleet.
Mentioning "Model Monitoring" in production is a big plus.
23. Zomato Full Stack Developer Interview Experience
Metadata: Zomato | Full Stack | SDE-1 | Medium
Skills: React Native, Node.js, AWS
Interview Experience
Heavy focus on Mobile performance. Since Zomato is mobile-first, they asked about optimizing
image loading, list virtualization in React Native, and handling "Offline Mode" for the delivery
app. In the backend round, I designed an API for "Restaurant Discovery" with various filters.
Takeaway: Know the mobile app lifecycle and how to minimize "Over-fetching" of data.
Mentioning GraphQL or efficient JSON structures for slow 3G networks shows you understand
the Indian market's constraints.
24. Razorpay Software Engineer Interview Experience

Metadata: Razorpay | Software Engineer | SDE-2 | Hard
Skills: Go, PHP, MySQL, Payment Gateways
Interview Experience
Very high bar for Backend engineering. I was asked to implement a "Deadlock-free Transaction"
system for moving money between two internal accounts. We did a deep dive into MySQL
"Isolation Levels" and how to handle "Partial Failures" in a distributed payment flow. Takeaway:
Transactional integrity is everything in Fintech. If your solution can lose even $0.01 in a race
condition, it's a fail. Practice designing systems that are "Eventual Consistent" but "Audit-ready."
25. Flipkart SDE Interview Experience
Metadata: Flipkart | SDE | SDE-1 | Hard
Skills: Java, Machine Coding, LLD, HLD
Interview Experience
The "Machine Coding" round is the ultimate filter. I had to build a "Parking Lot System" in 90
minutes—fully working code with extensible classes. This was followed by a DSA round on
Dynamic Programming (specifically 0/1 Knapsack variation) and Graphs. Takeaway: Practice
Low-Level Design (LLD) and SOLID principles religiously. If your code isn't modular or uses
"God Classes," you'll fail the machine coding round even if the logic is correct. Speed in writing
clean, bug-free code is the metric here.
26. Flipkart Data Analyst Interview Experience
Metadata: Flipkart | Data Analyst | Junior | Medium
Skills: Excel, SQL, Python, Business Analytics
Interview Experience
Heavy focus on Excel and SQL. I had to write complex nested queries to find "Customer
Retention" and "Churn" rates over a 6-month period, segmented by region. There was also a
"Guesstimate" round—e.g., "Estimate the number of smartphones sold in India during a Big
Billion Day sale." Takeaway: Be very comfortable with SQL Window functions. For the
guesstimates, it's not about the right number, but the logical structure of your assumptions.
Always break down the problem into smaller, manageable chunks.

27. Meesho Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Meesho | Backend Engineer | SDE-2 | Medium
Skills: Java, Spring Boot, Microservices, ElasticSearch
Interview Experience
The interview focused on "Scalable Discovery." How do you show millions of products to a user
in a personalized way without lagging? We discussed ElasticSearch indexing strategies and
how to handle "Write-heavy" workloads when sellers update prices. Coding was standard DSA
(Trees/Heaps). Takeaway: Understand search and ranking architectures. Meesho grows by
empowering small sellers, so discuss how you'd build "Reliable" and "Simple" systems that can
scale rapidly with minimal dev intervention.
28. CRED Full Stack Developer Interview Experience
Metadata: CRED | Full Stack | Senior | Hard
Skills: React, Node.js, Framer Motion, Design Systems
Interview Experience
CRED is obsessed with UI/UX. The interview involved building a complex, high-performance
animation in React (like a credit card flip or a sleek progress bar) and ensuring it worked at a
locked 60fps on mobile. The backend round focused on "Security" and "Data Encryption" for
sensitive card info. Takeaway: Focus on CSS animations and performance profiling. If you can
talk about the "Virtual DOM" vs "Direct DOM manipulation" for animations, you're in a good
spot. They want "Artists who can Code."
29. Zepto Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Zepto | Backend Engineer | SDE-1 | Medium
Skills: Node.js, PostGIS, Real-time Systems, Redis
Interview Experience
The focus was entirely on the "10-minute delivery" constraint. We discussed Geospatial queries
and how to use PostGIS to find the nearest delivery partner in milliseconds. I designed a "Slot

Booking" system that updates in real-time as delivery partners become available. Takeaway:
Study Geospatial indexing (R-trees/Quad-trees) and Redis. In the world of 10-minute delivery,
every millisecond of latency in the backend is a mile on the road. Show that you care about
"Throughput."
30. Dunzo Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Dunzo | Software Engineer | Junior | Medium
Skills: Kotlin, Python, Optimization Algorithms
Interview Experience
A mix of DSA and "Real-world Problem Solving." I was asked to solve a variation of the
"Traveling Salesman Problem" to optimize a multi-stop delivery route. The coding rounds were
focused on Arrays and HashMaps, with a follow-up on how to handle "Dynamic Pricing" during
high-demand hours. Takeaway: Focus on optimization and heuristics. They want to see that
you can take a complex math problem and turn it into a working script. Mentioning "Greedy
Algorithms" for route optimization is a good start.
31. NVIDIA ML Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: NVIDIA | ML Engineer | L3 | Hard
Skills: CUDA, C++, PyTorch, Kernels, Linear Algebra
Interview Experience
Deep dive into "GPU Architecture." I was asked how to optimize a matrix multiplication kernel in
CUDA to maximize "Shared Memory" usage. We also discussed the internal workings of
PyTorch's autograd engine and how to minimize latency in "Model Inference." Takeaway: You
need to know hardware. If you can't talk about memory coalescing, warp scheduling, or
TFLOPS, you aren't prepared. NVIDIA sits at the intersection of "Math" and "Metal"—you must
master both.
32. NVIDIA Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: NVIDIA | Software Engineer | SDE-2 | Medium

Skills: C, C++, Device Drivers, Linux Internals
Interview Experience
Focus on "System Software" and low-level C. Questions ranged from writing a simple character
driver to explaining how "Direct Memory Access" (DMA) works. I had to solve a coding problem
involving bit manipulation and custom memory allocators. Takeaway: Be a C expert.
Understand pointers, memory alignment, and the difference between stack and heap at a
granular level. They value engineers who aren't afraid of the "Kernel" and can debug at the
assembly level if needed.
33. Intel Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Intel | Software Engineer | Graduate | Medium
Skills: C, Assembly, Computer Architecture, Verilog
Interview Experience
The interview was heavy on "Computer Organization." I was asked about instruction pipelining,
branch prediction, and cache coherency protocols (MESI). One round involved writing a small
piece of Assembly code to perform an array sum. Takeaway: Refresh your Hennessy &
Patterson "Computer Architecture" textbook. Intel is all about "Silicon" and "Firmware." Knowing
how the CPU actually executes your C++ code is the key to passing their technical rounds.
34. Qualcomm Embedded Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Qualcomm | Embedded Engineer | Junior | Hard
Skills: Embedded C, RTOS, I2C/SPI, Microcontrollers
Interview Experience
Focus on "Real-time Constraints" and hardware protocols. I had to write code for a task
scheduler on a bare-metal system, ensuring that high-priority interrupts were handled within a
specific microsecond window. We discussed I2C vs SPI for sensor communication. Takeaway:
Know your communication protocols and "Interrupt Service Routines" (ISRs). Practice writing
"Safe" C code—no dynamic memory allocation in critical sections. Qualcomm is about
"Reliability" in the mobile and automotive space.

35. Oracle Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Oracle | Backend Engineer | IC2 | Medium
Skills: Java, SQL, Database Internals, Cloud Infra
Interview Experience
Very standard DSA and SQL, but the SQL was surprisingly deep. I had to explain "B-Tree vs
Hash Indexes" and how to optimize a query that was doing a full table scan. The Java round
focused on "Multi-threading" and the "Java Memory Model." Takeaway: Know how an RDBMS
works under the hood. Oracle is the "Database Company"—if you don't know what an
"Execution Plan" is or how "Acid Properties" are implemented, you'll struggle in their core
teams.
36. SAP Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: SAP | Software Engineer | Associate | Medium
Skills: Java, ABAP, Cloud Foundry, Distributed Systems
Interview Experience
Focus on "Enterprise Design Patterns." We discussed how to manage massive datasets across
distributed nodes while maintaining data integrity for financial systems. The coding round was
mostly about "Clean Code" and "Unit Testing." Takeaway: Focus on modularity. SAP builds
software that runs the world's biggest companies. They want engineers who write "Boring,
Reliable Code" that won't crash a multi-billion dollar supply chain. Use "Dependency Injection"
and "Interfaces" effectively.
37. Cisco Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Cisco | Software Engineer | Grade 4 | Medium
Skills: Networking (TCP/IP), Python, C++, Routing Protocols
Interview Experience
Every round had networking questions. "What happens when you type a URL in the browser?"
was a 30-minute deep dive into DNS, TCP handshakes, BGP, and ARP. Coding questions
involved implementing a "Network Packet Filter" using HashMaps. Takeaway: Master the OSI

model and the TCP/IP stack. Cisco is the "Backbone of the Internet." If you can't explain how a
"Router" differs from a "Switch" at the packet level, you're not ready. Brush up on Wireshark and
socket programming.
38. VMware Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: VMware | Backend Engineer | MTS-1 | Medium
Skills: Java, Virtualization, K8s, Distributed Systems
Interview Experience
Focus on "Virtualization" and "Cloud Native" technologies. I was asked how a Hypervisor
manages "CPU overcommitment" and how "Containers" differ from "VMs" in terms of kernel
sharing. Coding rounds focused on Trees and Graph traversals. Takeaway: Understand the
"Infrastructure" layer. VMware is moving heavily into Kubernetes (Tanzu), so knowing how K8s
"Control Planes" and "Data Planes" work will give you a significant advantage in the system
design rounds.
39. ServiceNow Software Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: ServiceNow | Software Engineer | Junior | Medium
Skills: JavaScript, Java, SaaS, Workflow Engines
Interview Experience
A mix of web technology and backend logic. I had to design a "Workflow Engine" where non-
technical users could drag-and-drop logic blocks. We discussed "Event-driven Architecture" and
how to handle thousands of concurrent automated tasks. Takeaway: Focus on "Extensibility."
ServiceNow is a platform for other businesses to build on. They look for engineers who can
build "Flexible" schemas and APIs that don't break when a customer adds a custom field.
40. Snowflake Backend Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Snowflake | Backend Engineer | Hard
Skills: C++, Database Internals, Storage Engines, Query Optimization

Interview Experience
One of the most difficult interviews I've experienced. Questions were on "Columnar Storage"
formats, "Micro-partitioning," and how to implement a "Lock-free Queue" in C++. We spent 45
minutes on "Query Plan Optimization." Takeaway: This is for true systems nerds. Study the
"Snowflake Paper" and general database research. They want people who can write high-
performance C++ that beats existing benchmarks. Know your "SIMD" instructions and "Cache-
conscious" programming.
41. Amazon DevOps Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Amazon | DevOps | L4 | Medium
Skills: AWS, Terraform, CI/CD, Shell Scripting
Interview Experience
Focus on "Infrastructure as Code" (IaC). I had to write a Terraform script to deploy a high-
availability VPC with public/private subnets and an Auto Scaling Group. We also discussed
"Blue-Green Deployment" strategies and how to rollback a failed production push. Takeaway:
Know the AWS "Well-Architected Framework." Amazon DevOps is about "Automation" and
"Self-healing." If you mention "Manual steps," you've already lost. Focus on "Monitoring" and
"Alerting" using CloudWatch.
42. Microsoft Cloud Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Microsoft | Cloud Engineer | Medium
Skills: Azure, PowerShell, ARM Templates, Networking
Interview Experience
Heavy focus on Azure-specific services like Azure Functions, CosmosDB, and Virtual Networks.
I was asked how to secure a "Public API" using Azure API Management and OAuth2. Technical
rounds also included basic C# for writing serverless functions. Takeaway: Get an Azure
certification before applying. They expect you to know the difference between "LRS, GRS, and
ZRS" storage and when to use which. Microsoft values "Security-first" cloud design.

43. Google Cloud Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Google | Cloud Engineer | Hard
Skills: GCP, Kubernetes (GKE), Anthos, Networking
Interview Experience
Focused on "Hybrid Cloud" and "Containerization." I was asked how to migrate a legacy on-
prem app to GKE using a "Strangler Pattern." We discussed "Service Meshes" (Istio) and how
to manage traffic splitting. Takeaway: Master GKE. Google is Kubernetes. If you don't
understand "Pods," "Deployments," and "Ingress," you won't pass. Also, be ready for "Site
Reliability" (SRE) questions—Google invented the term, after all.
44. Oracle Cloud Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: Oracle | Cloud Engineer | Medium
Skills: OCI, Networking, Linux, Terraform
Interview Experience
Focus on "Bare Metal" cloud performance and low-latency networking. I had to design a
network topology for a high-performance database cluster. We discussed "VCN Peering" and
"FastConnect." Takeaway: Understand OCI's unique networking layers and how they differ
from AWS. Oracle markets itself as the best cloud for "Heavy Databases," so show that you
understand "IOPS" and "Network Latency" optimization.
45. IBM DevOps Engineer Interview Experience
Metadata: IBM | DevOps | Junior | Medium
Skills: Ansible, Jenkins, OpenShift, Linux
Interview Experience
Focus on "Enterprise Automation" using Jenkins and Ansible for legacy systems. I was asked
how to automate the patching of 500 Linux servers without downtime. We also discussed
"OpenShift" as a managed K8s platform. Takeaway: Know your "Configuration Management."
IBM works with many "Legacy" clients, so showing you can bridge the gap between "Old-school
Mainframes" and "Modern Cloud" is a massive advantage.

46. Fintech Startup – Backend Engineer
Metadata: Fintech | Backend | SDE-2 | Hard
Skills: Go, PostgreSQL, Ledger Design, Distributed Transactions
Interview Experience
Focus on "Idempotency" and "Data Consistency." How do you ensure a payment isn't
processed twice during a network retry? I designed a "Double-entry Bookkeeping" ledger from
scratch in the system design round. Takeaway: Study distributed transactions and the "Saga
Pattern." In Fintech, "Eventual Consistency" is often not enough. You must understand how to
use "Postgres Locks" and "Unique Constraints" to prevent financial discrepancies.
47. AI Startup – ML Engineer
Metadata: AI Startup | ML Engineer | Medium
Skills: Python, LangChain, OpenAI API, Vector DBs
Interview Experience
Focus on "RAG" (Retrieval Augmented Generation). I had to build a simple Q&A bot over a set
of PDF documents using LangChain and Pinecone. We discussed "Prompt Engineering" and
how to reduce "Hallucinations" in LLMs. Takeaway: Stay updated on Generative AI. They want
to see that you can take a raw LLM and build a "Product" around it. Mentioning "Evaluation
Frameworks" for LLM outputs is a huge plus.
48. SaaS Startup – Full Stack Developer
Metadata: SaaS Startup | Full Stack | Junior | Medium
Skills: Next.js, Prisma, Tailwind, TypeScript
Interview Experience
Rapid-fire coding. I had to build a "Subscription Dashboard" with real-time billing updates
(Stripe integration) in 60 minutes. They cared about my ability to move fast and "Ship" a
working feature. Takeaway: Speed and "Product Sense" are key. In a SaaS startup, they don't

have time for 5 rounds of LeetCode. They want to see if you can take a "User Story" and turn it
into a "Pull Request" by the end of the day.
49. EdTech Startup – Frontend Engineer
Metadata: EdTech | Frontend | Medium
Skills: React, Video Players, Canvas API, WebSockets
Interview Experience
Focus on "Interactive UI." How do you build a custom video player that allows teachers to draw
on the screen in real-time? We discussed using the "Canvas API" and "WebRTC" for low-
latency video streaming. Takeaway: Know your "Web APIs" beyond just React. EdTech is about
engagement, so show that you can build "Fun" and "Performant" interfaces that work on slow
student tablets.
50. HealthTech Startup – Backend Engineer
Metadata: HealthTech | Backend | Medium
Skills: Python, HIPAA Compliance, FHIR, Encryption
Interview Experience
Heavy focus on "Data Privacy" and "Regulatory Compliance." I was asked how to encrypt
"Personally Identifiable Information" (PII) at rest and in transit. We discussed the "FHIR"
standard for medical data exchange. Takeaway: Know your security. In HealthTech, a data leak
is a legal disaster. Show that you prioritize "Security" over "Speed" and understand "Audit Logs"
and "Access Control" (RBAC).
51. Gaming Company – Backend Engineer
Metadata: Gaming | Backend | Hard
Skills: C++, UDP, WebSockets, Redis
Interview Experience

Focus on "Latency" and "Synchronization." How do you sync player positions across 100 clients
in a fast-paced shooter? We discussed "UDP" vs "TCP" and "Client-side Prediction."
Takeaway: Study networking for games. You need to understand how to handle "Packet Loss"
and "Jitter." Mentioning "Entity-Component-System" (ECS) architecture for the backend is a
great way to show you speak their language.
52. Cybersecurity Firm – Software Engineer
Metadata: CyberSec | Software Engineer | Hard
Skills: C, Assembly, Reverse Engineering, Python
Interview Experience
I was given a compiled binary and asked to find a "Buffer Overflow" vulnerability. Then, I had to
write a Python script to automate the detection of similar patterns in a large codebase.
Takeaway: Think like a hacker. They want to see that you understand how "Exploits" work so
you can build "Defenses." Knowledge of "Static vs Dynamic Analysis" and "Fuzzing" is
essential.
53. PayPal – Software Engineer
Metadata: PayPal | SWE | Junior | Medium
Skills: Java, Spring Boot, REST, SQL
Interview Experience
Standard Java/Spring questions. I was asked to "Explain Spring Bean Scopes" and "How does
@Transactional work?" Coding was LeetCode Medium—mostly Arrays and Strings. Takeaway:
Be strong in your Java fundamentals. PayPal has a massive legacy Java codebase, so they
value engineers who understand the "Spring Ecosystem" and can write clean, testable Java
code.
54. PayPal – Backend Engineer
Metadata: PayPal | Backend | SDE-2 | Medium
Skills: Node.js, Microservices, API Gateways

Interview Experience
Focus on "API Design" and "Rate Limiting." How do you build a gateway that handles millions
of requests while protecting internal services from being overwhelmed? We discussed "Circuit
Breakers" (Hystrix). Takeaway: Know your microservice patterns. PayPal is moving towards a
more "Modern" stack, so show that you understand "Service Discovery" and "Distributed
Tracing" (Zipkin/Jaeger).
55. Bloomberg – Software Engineer
Metadata: Bloomberg | SWE | New Grad | Hard
Skills: C++, Data Structures, Memory Management
Interview Experience
Bloomberg loves C++. Expect deep questions on "Smart Pointers," std::vector  internal
resizing, and "Virtual Functions." My coding problem was a variation of the "Trading Order
Book" (using Heaps and Maps). Takeaway: Practice LeetCode in C++. They don't just want the
answer; they want to see that you understand "Memory Layout" and how to write code that
doesn't cause "Cache Misses."
56. Bloomberg – Data Scientist
Metadata: Bloomberg | Data Scientist | Medium
Skills: Python, Time Series, NLP, Finance
Interview Experience
Focus on "Financial Sentiment Analysis." I had to design a model that reads "Twitter Feeds"
and predicts stock market volatility. We discussed "LSTM" vs "Transformers" for time-series
data. Takeaway: Know your NLP. Bloomberg's core product is "Information," so show that you
can extract "Signal" from "Noise" in massive text datasets.
57. Goldman Sachs – Software Engineer
Metadata: Goldman Sachs | SWE | Analyst | Medium

Skills: Java, Algorithms, Hashing, Math
Interview Experience
Focus on "Mathematical DSA." I had many questions on Number Theory (Primes, GCD) and
"Combinatorics." The coding round involved finding the "First non-repeating character" in a
stream of millions of characters. Takeaway: Practice math-heavy coding problems. Goldman
values "Logical Rigor." They also have a behavioral round focused on "Integrity" and
"Teamwork"—don't treat it as a formality.
58. JPMorgan – Software Engineer
Metadata: JPMorgan | SWE | Analyst | Medium
Skills: Java, SQL, Agile, Spring
Interview Experience
Very behavioral and process-oriented. "Tell me about a time you handled a conflict in a group."
The technical rounds were basic Java/Spring and SQL (Joins/Indexes). Takeaway: Be a "Team
Player." JPMC is a massive organization where "Agile" and "Communication" are as important
as your coding skills. Show that you can work within a large corporate structure.
59. Morgan Stanley – Software Engineer
Metadata: Morgan Stanley | SWE | Junior | Medium
Skills: C++, Java, Linux, Multi-threading
Interview Experience
Questions on "Low-latency" and "JVM Tuning." I was asked how to reduce "Garbage Collection"
pauses in a high-frequency trading app. Coding was mostly about "Linked Lists" and "Queues."
Takeaway: Know your "Operating Systems." Morgan Stanley values engineers who understand
"Context Switching" and "CPU Pinning" to squeeze every millisecond of performance out of
their systems.
60. Deloitte – Software Engineer

Metadata: Deloitte | SWE | Consultant | Medium
Skills: .NET, SQL, Communication, SDLC
Interview Experience
More focus on "Consulting Skills." How would you explain a "Cloud Migration" strategy to a non-
technical CEO? The technical test was a mix of .NET basics and SQL. Takeaway:
Communication is 50% of the interview. They hire consultants who happen to code. Show that
you can gather "Requirements" and turn them into a "Technical Spec."
61. Accenture – Full Stack Developer
Metadata: Accenture | Full Stack | Junior | Medium
Skills: JavaScript, React, SQL, Java
Interview Experience
Standard tech rounds. I had to build a simple "Employee Management System" using React
and a Java backend. They checked for "Full Stack" versatility. Takeaway: Be a "Generalist."
Accenture moves people between projects frequently, so show that you are willing to learn new
languages (e.g., "I know React, but I'm happy to learn Angular for a client").
62. Infosys – Software Engineer
Metadata: Infosys | SWE | Entry | Easy
Skills: Java/Python, Aptitude, Basic DBMS
Interview Experience
Focus on the "Aptitude Test" (Logical Reasoning, Quant). The technical interview was very
basic: "Difference between Class and Object" and "Reverse a string." Takeaway: Don't fail the
aptitude test—it's the biggest filter. In the HR round, just show that you are enthusiastic and
willing to relocate to any office.
63. TCS – Software Engineer

Metadata: TCS | SWE | Digital Role | Medium
Skills: Python, DSA, DBMS, Cloud Basics
Interview Experience
The "Digital" track has decent DSA questions (Medium level). I was asked about "Inheritance"
and had to write a SQL query for "Second Highest Salary." Takeaway: Aim for the "Digital" or
"Prime" track for better pay. These roles require a solid understanding of one programming
language and basic system design.
64. Wipro – Software Engineer
Metadata: Wipro | SWE | Entry | Easy
Skills: C/C++, Basic Web, Logic
Interview Experience
Standard campus placement flow. Basic coding test followed by a technical interview on "Final
year project." HR round was about "Hobbies" and "Bond agreement." Takeaway: Be confident
and clear in your communication. Show that you have a strong grasp of your college projects,
as they will ask most of their questions from there.
